narrative: As a Black male student applying to law school, you've shared your academic journey, including your Year 1 grades placing you in the 6th decile at your school—solidly above average—and your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, which is a bit below the typical 3.21 seen among past applicants. Your LSAT score of 32 also falls short of the average 36.7, and coming from an upper-middle-class family while planning to attend part-time sets your background apart from most full-time applicants. The admissions model evaluated all this to predict your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimating a 50% probability of failure—right on the edge, but enough to tip the decision toward rejection to minimize the school's risk of lower bar passage rates. The key factor driving this prediction was your Year 3 grades, which landed you in the 3rd decile—well below the average 5th or 6th decile—strongly signaling to the model a heightened risk of struggling on the bar. This stood out as the most influential element, pushing the prediction firmly toward failure more than anything else in your profile. On a more positive note, your part-time status actually worked in your favor, as the model sees fewer part-time students succeeding but views it as less of a drag here, nudging the odds slightly toward passing compared to the typical full-time path. Similarly, your stronger Year 1 performance provided a helpful counterbalance, pulling the prediction a bit away from failure since it exceeded the average decile. Overall, your Year 3 dip outweighed these strengths, especially when stacked against peers who maintain consistent or higher deciles throughout, making your profile riskier in the model's eyes. Many successful applicants hover in higher deciles across the board, with stronger LSATs to boot, which is where you differed most sharply. To move forward, focus on boosting your recent academic performance—perhaps through targeted tutoring or retaking key courses—to climb those deciles, and consider prepping intensively for a higher LSAT retake. Retake the LSAT if possible, as even a few points could shift your profile dramatically. With your upper-middle background and part-time flexibility, you're well-positioned to build a stronger case; reach out to advisors for personalized prep plans, and reapply once you've addressed these gaps—many students like you turn predictions around with focused effort.
